video_title: Making Sense Of A Strange Jobs Report
gist: Ed Yardeni argues the June US jobs report was statistically noisy but not recessionary (payrolls +57k, unemployment 4.2%), reads the labor slowdown as supply-side (retiring baby boomers, tighter immigration), and stays firmly bullish on stocks: he sees more FIMO (fabulous earnings momentum) than FOMO and targets S&P 500 ~8,250 by year-end on 2027 forward earnings near $400 and a ~20 multiple, while flagging that any 'bubble' risk sits on the earnings (E) side, not valuations. Note: he dates the recording July 6, 2026 despite the 2026-07-10 upload date.

insights.claim: Oil prices have tumbled even though the Strait of Hormuz is not back to normal throughput (Chinese and some Indian vessels getting through), because the pre-war bear market driven by Chinese EV adoption and a weak Chinese economy has reasserted itself after the ceasefire.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A structural, demand-driven oil bear case (not just geopolitics) supports a disinflationary regime and lower energy-sector earnings estimates.
insights.quote: as soon as uh we got uh I don't know what it is it's a ceasefire it's a pause uh whatever it is the price of oil came tumbling down

insights.claim: China is in roughly the fifth year of a severe property recession/depression that policymakers have not meaningfully addressed, leaving big-saver Chinese households trapped in illiquid real estate and rotating into gold and Chinese equities as trading vehicles rather than durable investments.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A prolonged China property bust caps global commodity demand and Chinese consumption, reinforcing the disinflation and weak-oil thesis.
insights.quote: they're I think in the five fifth year of a pretty nasty property recession depression and nothing's really been done to alleviate that pressure

insights.claim: Gold peaked near $5,500 before the war, fell to its 200-day moving average and then to $4,000, which Yardeni expects to hold as support within a rising channel, even as a more hawkish Fed and stronger dollar pose headwinds.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: $4,000 gold is the key technical line to watch; a hold implies a base for renewed upside despite hawkish-Fed pressure.
insights.quote: it uh it came down from 5,500 down to its 200 day moving average and then it got down to 4,000

insights.claim: The war caused gold to fall rather than rise because emerging-market central banks like Turkey, hit by currency declines and cut-off oil, had to stop buying and sell gold to defend their currencies.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Explains why gold behaved counter-intuitively during conflict; EM central-bank flows, not just rates, drive gold at the margin.
insights.quote: countries like Turkey had to sell gold uh certainly stop buying gold but also sell gold

insights.claim: Copper is above $6 in a large rally that has drawn little attention, driven by the AI trade and copper demand in electronics.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Copper's under-noticed strength is a real-economy confirmation of AI capex; a way to play the AI buildout outside semis.
insights.quote: Copper's over six $6. It's kind of a amazing rally that nobody's paid much attention to, but obviously has a lot to do uh with the AI trade

insights.claim: Yardeni frames the market as driven more by FIMO ('fabulous earnings momentum') than FOMO, arguing that if irrational exuberance exists it is on the earnings (E) side via too-optimistic analysts, not on the valuation (P/E) side.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Reframes bubble risk: watch for earnings-estimate disappointment rather than multiple compression as the trigger for a selloff.
insights.quote: for now um I see a lot more FIMO than FOMO. Fimo being our uh uh our coinage for the idea of uh fabulous earnings momentum

insights.claim: S&P 500 first-quarter earnings were very strong and analysts expect the second, third and fourth quarters of the year to each be up more than 20% year-over-year.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Double-digit-plus quarterly EPS growth is the fundamental engine underpinning the bullish index target.
insights.quote: the the second, third, and fourth quarters of this year are expected to be up more than 20% per quarter on a on a year-over-year basis

insights.claim: Analyst 2027 operating-earnings estimates for the S&P 500 have risen to about $402 per share (versus roughly $375 currently), and applying a ~20 multiple to a rounded $400 implies the S&P 500 near 8,000, with Yardeni's explicit year-end target of 8,250 (about 10% upside over six months).
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Concrete, model-driven year-end index target (8,250) with the earnings and multiple inputs laid out for tracking.
insights.quote: they're up to $42 uh a share

insights.claim: Yardeni concedes he has not been bullish enough on earnings historically and thinks the S&P could exceed 8,000 by year-end if 2027 estimates top $400 or the multiple expands to 21-22.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Signals upside skew to his own target; his base case may be conservative relative to his true view.
insights.quote: I I actually haven't been bullish enough and I've been among the bulls all in all

insights.claim: June US payrolls rose 57,000 and the three-month average fell to 111,000 (down from 188,000 before this report), which Yardeni notes is still roughly double the ~50,000 break-even payroll number cited only months earlier.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Even a 'disappointing' headline is above break-even, supporting the no-recession base case despite a softer trend.
insights.quote: the month over month uh was 57,000

insights.claim: The sharp drop in leisure and hospitality employment is likely a statistical anomaly (Memorial Day falling earlier in May pulling hiring forward, plus FIFA-related staffing timing) and is a candidate to be reversed in later reports.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Implies the weak print overstates labor softness; expect an upward revision/rebound, keeping the soft-landing narrative intact.
insights.quote: this uh this looks like it's a candidate for being reversed

insights.claim: Growth in professional and business services, including the administrative and support roles supposedly most exposed to AI, suggests AI is not yet destroying jobs and may be helping workers with AI skills keep or find work.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Undercuts the AI-job-destruction narrative; supportive of continued consumer/labor resilience.
insights.quote: administrative and support services. Uh those are the people that are supposed to get replaced by AI. Well maybe that's not the case

insights.claim: The unemployment rate dipped to 4.2% for 'not great reasons' — the labor force shrank by more than employment did — reflecting supply-side dynamics (fewer people looking for work) rather than weakening labor demand.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A supply-driven labor slowdown keeps unemployment low and shifts Fed focus to inflation, not employment.
insights.quote: it dipped down to 4.2% and yes uh it was for not great reasons the labor force dropped by more than uh employment dropped

insights.claim: Falling labor-force participation is driven largely by baby boomers retiring into their 70s and 80s, compounded by younger workers puzzlingly dropping out and declining foreign-born labor force due to tighter immigration enforcement.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A structurally shrinking labor supply supports low unemployment and persistent wage floors but constrains growth capacity.
insights.quote: the participation rate is partly because of retiring baby boomers, but it looks like younger people are also dropping out

insights.claim: Using Powell's supply-versus-demand labor framework (supply = labor force; demand = household employment plus job openings), the labor market is roughly in balance and demand has ticked back slightly above supply, so the Fed's concern rightly belongs on the inflation side of its mandate.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A balanced labor market removes the employment-mandate case for cuts and keeps policy tilted toward inflation vigilance.
insights.quote: the demand uh for labor minus the supply of labor has turned back up a little bit

insights.claim: June CPI may come in around -0.1% because of falling gasoline prices, which combined with 3.5% wage growth could produce roughly a 0.4% rise in Yardeni's earned-income proxy (a wages/salaries/personal-income measure) at an all-time record high.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A negative monthly CPI print plus record real earned income supports both disinflation and consumer-spending durability.
insights.quote: the uh inflation number for June may very well be something like close to minus 0.1% because of the drop in gasoline prices

insights.claim: Yardeni names productivity as the most positive reason to expect durable disinflation, alongside falling oil, a housing plateau and Chinese EVs, and cites a Dallas Fed working paper finding unauthorized immigration drove a 30% surge in local home prices from 2021 to 2024.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Productivity-led disinflation is the bullish 'roaring 20s' underpinning that lets earnings grow without forcing the Fed tighter.
insights.quote: I would throw in productivity as the most positive reason to believe that this inflation uh is is possible

insights.claim: With Kevin Warsh as the new Fed chair having pivoted to a hawkish stance, Yardeni keeps a July 25-basis-point rate hike 'on the table' but says it is no longer his highest-conviction call, and suspects the surprise could be that conditions calm enough that Warsh need not do much.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A hawkish-but-possibly-restrained Fed is a key swing factor; a 'no-hike' surprise would be a tailwind for gold and equities.
insights.quote: our 25 basis point rate hike in July is still on the table but it's not our you know highest conviction bet at this point

insights.claim: From a contrarian view, Yardeni welcomes the surge in 'bubble' talk from perma-bears calling AI the biggest bubble ever, noting that prior SPAC and meme-stock bubbles burst a couple years ago without triggering anything terrible.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Widespread bubble anxiety during a melt-up is a contrarian bullish signal; supports staying long.
insights.quote: from a contrarian perspective, I'm glad to to see that people are worrying about bubbles

insights.claim: The information-technology forward P/E, which hit about 55 in the prior tech bubble, has come down not because tech stocks crashed but because earnings (FIMO) rose, so tech no longer looks particularly expensive unless the market disbelieves the earnings forecasts.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Investors' unwillingness to pay up for tech's forecast earnings shows valuation cushion, not froth, on the P/E side.
insights.quote: it's come down not because the stocks have crashed but because uh earnings because of FIMO

insights.claim: Long-term earnings-growth (LTEG) expectations compiled by IBES are 25.5% for the S&P 500 overall but 43.5% for information technology (versus 28% in the prior cycle), which Yardeni flags as a possible locus of irrational exuberance on the earnings side.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: 43.5% IT long-term growth is a specific overheating gauge to monitor for the earnings-side bubble he warns about.
insights.quote: when you look at LTEG for the information technology, it's off off the charts at 43.5 uh compared to 28 back here

insights.claim: Semiconductor forward profit margins have risen to about 50% and could be due for a surprising drop, though Yardeni doubts a sudden margin collapse because it would require excess capacity that is hard to bring on given how difficult the fabs are to build; notably investors are paying less and less for that margin.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A 50% semi margin is the key fragility to watch, but investors' shrinking willingness to pay for it provides valuation cushion.
insights.quote: the profit margin uh forward profit margin really took off and now it's at 50

insights.claim: Yardeni acknowledges the earnings-quality concern that some S&P momentum reflects capex spending by other index members plus one-time mark-to-market capital gains booked as other income, but argues analysts will not extrapolate those one-time gains, so it is a 'realistic issue' rather than fatal.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Flags that index EPS may be partly circular/one-time; a reason to scrutinize free-cash-flow-based earnings normalization.
insights.quote: It's not wall of worry fodder. It's realistic issues.

insights.claim: Yardeni sees the market broadening beyond the Magnificent 7 to the other 493 stocks and into SMID-caps, with the Russell 2000 breaking out to new highs and Dow theory bullish (both Dow transports and industrials at record highs); the equal-weight-versus-market-weight trend that reversed during the war is resuming toward breadth.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Broadening breadth favors equal-weight and small/mid-cap exposure over concentrated mega-cap bets.
insights.quote: we we think the market is broadening to the the 493 and it's broadening out to this smid smidcaps

insights.claim: Year-to-date the X-Mags (S&P 500 ex-Magnificent 7) has outperformed the Magnificent 7 (MAGS), and Yardeni notes rotation within tech away from the MAG 7 and software into semiconductors, though he expects possible reversal and stays market-weight tech.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: The 493 leading the MAG 7 YTD supports diversifying away from mega-cap tech; watch semis-vs-software rotation.
insights.quote: year to date we're way ahead with the X-Mags compared to the to to the MAGS

insights.claim: Yardeni's favored/overweight sectors are financials (banks breaking out to new highs, though investment banks stalling), healthcare (biotech looking strong, pharma improving), and industrials, while he remains only market-weight in technology.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Explicit sector allocation: overweight financials/healthcare/industrials, neutral tech — actionable positioning tilts.
insights.quote: financials is one of our overweight uh sectors

insights.claim: Yardeni views the newly launched Trump accounts (effective July 4) as relatively small in near-term equity-market impact but great long term, tying them to his 2021 book 'In Praise of Profits' proposal that the government seed S&P 500 accounts for every baby born in America.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A structural, long-duration retail bid into equities via seeded index accounts supports the secular bull case.
insights.quote: initially it's uh it's relatively small, but uh I think it uh long in long term I think it's great
